class_schedule.topic: Introduction to Operations Management and Process Design
learning_objectives: Define operations management and its strategic role
Identify the transformation process: inputs, transformation, outputs
Classify process types: project, job shop, batch, assembly line, continuous

lecture_notes.introduction: Operations management is about designing and controlling the production of goods and services. Every organization performs operations, and doing them well is a competitive advantage.
key_concepts: Operations as a transformation process
Goods vs. services: tangibility, standardization, customer contact
Process strategy: process focus, repetitive, product focus
Productivity and multi-factor productivity measures
Operations strategy alignment with business strategy
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping the operations process for Amazon fulfillment, from order to delivery
setup_requirements: Textbook: Heizer & Render, Operations Management (13th ed.)

review.value: Converting inputs (materials, labor, capital) into outputs (goods and services) that create value for customers
Output divided by input; a key measure of operational efficiency

learning_objectives: Distinguish between qualitative and quantitative forecasting methods
Apply moving average and exponential smoothing techniques
Measure forecast accuracy using MAD and MAPE

lecture_notes.introduction: Forecasting drives virtually every operations decision. Production planning, inventory management, staffing, and budgeting all depend on accurate demand forecasts.
key_concepts: Qualitative methods: Delphi, market research, executive opinion
Time series components: trend, seasonality, cyclical, random
Moving average and weighted moving average
Exponential smoothing: Ft+1 = alpha*At + (1-alpha)*Ft
Forecast error measures: MAD, MSE, MAPE
lecture_notes.demonstration: Forecasting quarterly sales using real data: comparing simple moving average, exponential smoothing, and trend-adjusted methods

review.value: Weighted average giving more weight to recent observations; alpha controls responsiveness vs. stability
Mean Absolute Deviation: average of absolute forecast errors; measures typical forecast miss in same units as data

learning_objectives: Apply break-even analysis to capacity decisions
Design effective facility layouts for different process types
readings: Facility Layout Case Study: Toyota Production System

lecture_notes.introduction: Capacity decisions are among the most important strategic choices in operations. Too little capacity means lost sales; too much means wasted resources.
key_concepts: Design capacity vs. effective capacity vs. actual output
Utilization = actual output / design capacity
Efficiency = actual output / effective capacity
Break-even analysis for capacity expansion
Layout types: process, product, cellular, fixed-position
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a facility layout for a manufacturing cell using systematic layout planning

review.value: The percentage of available capacity being used; helps identify bottlenecks and expansion needs
Groups dissimilar machines into cells to process families of parts with similar requirements

learning_objectives: Apply ABC analysis for inventory classification
Calculate Economic Order Quantity and reorder points
Evaluate holding costs, ordering costs, and stockout costs

lecture_notes.introduction: Inventory is one of the most expensive assets a company holds. Effective inventory management balances the cost of holding too much against the risk of having too little.
key_concepts: ABC analysis: 80/20 rule applied to inventory
EOQ = sqrt(2DS/H) where D=demand, S=setup cost, H=holding cost
Reorder point = demand during lead time + safety stock
Holding costs vs. ordering costs tradeoff
Just-in-time (JIT) philosophy and implementation
lecture_notes.demonstration: EOQ calculation and sensitivity analysis for a retail distributor, including safety stock determination

review.value: Minimizes total inventory cost by finding the order quantity where holding cost equals ordering cost
Extra inventory held to protect against variability in demand or lead time

lecture_notes.introduction: Quality is not just an inspection activity; it is a philosophy that must be embedded in every process. Statistical tools give managers the data to manage quality proactively.
key_concepts: Deming's 14 points and continuous improvement
Cost of quality: prevention, appraisal, internal failure, external failure
Statistical process control: X-bar and R charts
Six Sigma DMAIC: Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control
Process capability: Cp and Cpk indices
lecture_notes.demonstration: Constructing X-bar and R control charts from production sample data and identifying out-of-control signals

review.value: A time-ordered plot with center line and control limits (UCL/LCL) that distinguishes common cause from special cause variation
Define the problem, Measure current performance, Analyze root causes, Improve the process, Control to sustain gains

learning_objectives: Identify the seven wastes in lean manufacturing
Apply lean tools: value stream mapping, 5S, kanban
Compare lean, agile, and leagile approaches

lecture_notes.introduction: Lean thinking, pioneered by Toyota, has transformed operations management. Its principles of waste elimination and continuous improvement apply to manufacturing, services, and knowledge work.
key_concepts: Seven wastes: overproduction, waiting, transport, overprocessing, inventory, motion, defects
Value stream mapping: current state and future state
5S: Sort, Set in order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain
Kanban pull systems
Kaizen events and continuous improvement culture
lecture_notes.demonstration: Value stream mapping exercise for a pizza delivery process, identifying and eliminating waste

review.value: Create maximum value for the customer while minimizing waste; every step must add value or be eliminated
A pull-based scheduling system where production is triggered by actual demand rather than forecasts

questions.question: Given quarterly demand of 100, 120, 110, and 130, calculate the forecast for the next quarter using: (a) a 4-period simple moving average, and (b) exponential smoothing with alpha=0.3, starting forecast of 105.

A factory has a design capacity of 10,000 units/week and effective capacity of 8,500 units/week. Actual output last week was 7,200 units. Calculate utilization and efficiency, and explain which metric is more useful for the operations manager.
questions.answer: Utilization = 7,200/10,000 = 72%. Efficiency = 7,200/8,500 = 84.7%. Efficiency is more useful for the operations manager because it measures performance against what is realistically achievable (accounting for planned downtime and maintenance), whereas utilization includes capacity that is not practically available.

rubric.key: Explains which is more useful with reasoning

questions.question: Annual demand is 10,000 units, ordering cost is $50/order, and holding cost is $2/unit/year. Calculate the EOQ and the total annual inventory cost at EOQ.
questions.answer: EOQ = sqrt(2 x 10,000 x 50 / 2) = sqrt(500,000) = 707 units. Annual ordering cost = (10,000/707) x $50 = $707. Annual holding cost = (707/2) x $2 = $707. Total = $707 + $707 = $1,414.

rubric.key: Notes that holding and ordering costs are equal at EOQ

questions.question: Explain the difference between common cause variation and special cause variation in a production process, and describe how a control chart helps distinguish between them.
questions.answer: Common cause variation is inherent in the process and affects all output (e.g., slight temperature fluctuations). Special cause variation is due to identifiable, assignable factors outside normal operation (e.g., tool breakage, untrained operator). A control chart plots measurements over time with statistically calculated upper and lower control limits. Points within the limits represent common cause variation; points outside the limits or patterns (runs, trends) signal special cause variation requiring investigation.

rubric.key: Explains how control chart distinguishes them

major_assignments.title: Operations Process Improvement Project
major_assignments.description: Identify a real operational process (at a local business, campus facility, or workplace) and conduct a systematic improvement analysis using lean tools, process mapping, and quality management techniques.
objectives: Map and analyze a real operational process
Propose quantified improvements using operations tools
Present findings in a professional consulting-style report
deliverables: Future-state value stream map with improvements
Written report (8-10 pages) with implementation recommendations

criteria: Data collected through observation and measurement
Root cause analysis for key issues
Thorough process analysis with real data

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of operations concepts and quantitative techniques

problem_sets.description: Weekly quantitative problems covering forecasting, inventory, and quality

major_assignments.description: Process improvement project and operations analysis

midterm_examination.description: Covers process design, forecasting, capacity, and inventory management

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including quality management and lean operations

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance required. In-class exercises contribute to participation grade and cannot be made up.
course_policies.late_submissions: Problem sets penalized 10% per day. Process improvement project milestones have firm deadlines.
course_policies.collaboration: Process improvement project may be completed in teams of 2-3. Problem sets are individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All data in process improvement project must be genuinely collected. Fabricating observations will result in course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Excel or statistical software required. Access to process mapping tools (Visio, Lucidchart, or equivalent).
